author: terencechain
content: i think the best strategy is DC8...  split the batch into 8 sub batches and recurse into each that fails, down to size 1. Even with 8 bad signatures. i can verify everything under 2s

author: terencechain
content: are we going with 8192? or a smaller number? or TBD?

author: jtraglia
content: <@363800010518822915> potuz suggested we raise the limit. Might need to test 16384 too. That would correspond to ~400m gas.

author: potuz
content: The biggest problem is that the attack is essentially free. It's just the gas spent unless we act fast to slash them

author: potuz
content: You need to have a lot of capital but you recover it

author: potuz
content: So any large group can trigger it

author: jtraglia
content: Couldn't a malicious builder include deposit requests with invalid signatures for free? Up to whatever the limit is.

author: tersec
content: as far as SSZ limits, EIP-7688 addresses this already: https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-7688#converted-types

author: jtraglia
content: So the `AllBad` case might need to be taken serious.

author: tersec
content: And it's already CFI'd for Glamsterdam, it just hasn't been applied yet due to not being a headliner

author: tersec
content: So if the problem to be solved is, increase the SSZ limits, that's going to happen naturally

author: potuz
content: Invalid signatures is not an attack

author: potuz
content: Valid ones are

author: potuz
content: I don't really care about someone that can burn 8K ETH per block

author: potuz
content: The problem is that you can use 8K ETH and withdraw it in a few epochs

author: jtraglia
content: Is this because these would be rejected before the signature checks?

author: potuz
content: No, because invalid signature deposits are burned money

author: potuz
content: The EL doesn't return it

author: jtraglia
content: Either way. $20m really is not _that_ much to a company/attacker/wealthy_person/nation_state.

author: potuz
content: Valid ones are the problem

author: potuz
content: And what I'm arguing is that you don't need to "spend" 20M

author: potuz
content: You just need to have it

author: potuz
content: The attack is free

author: jtraglia
content: Yes, that's what I'm saying too.

author: jtraglia
content: Okay sorry. We agree.

author: jtraglia
content: But even then, $20m burned to cause a big network issue? Could be worth it.

author: jtraglia
content: Would this just cause a few missed slots or worse?

author: jtraglia
content: Say it takes 20 seconds to verify 8k invalid signatures.

author: potuz
content: It's not burned!

author: potuz
content: You recover it

author: potuz
content: So you trigger it again

author: potuz
content: And if you have 80m you do it constantly

author: potuz
content: Every single slot

author: potuz
content: Probably need a few more millions but you get the point

author: potuz
content: Gas is cheap, cause you don't need to compete for inclusion

author: jtraglia
content: Didn't you just say it's burned here?

author: potuz
content: Only on invalid ones

author: potuz
content: If I attack I'll send valid ones

author: jtraglia
content: Verifying invalid signatures is slower. That is what I'm talking about here.

author: potuz
content: And withdraw

author: jtraglia
content: Yeah I understand that attack too.

author: potuz
content: You only need to send one invalid and 8K valid if you want to grief that

author: jtraglia
content: But according to <@363800010518822915>, that might only take a few seconds to verify. It's not even an attack.

author: potuz
content: More than 2 seconds reorgs the block

author: jtraglia
content: 8191 valid + 1 invalid is still just 1.23 seconds.

author: potuz
content: That's more than enough to reorg most blocks

author: potuz
content: And the problem is that the next block needs to include the same requests

author: potuz
content: Forever

author: jtraglia
content: Then why are you suggesting we raise the limit?

author: potuz
content: Because invalidating the Payload seems to be even worse

author: potuz
content: Then there's not even a chance of recovering unless a builder doesn't pack the txs

author: potuz
content: With this we only need the next proposer not to reorg the block

author: potuz
content: Which I think will happen if we stick to the current honest reorg feature of only reorging one slot

author: potuz
content: So the first block will be reorged but the second one won't

author: potuz
content: Will take seconds to execute but the third proposer should build on the previous block

author: potuz
content: So I think we should eventually heal

author: potuz
content: The problem is that the attack can be always continued

author: jtraglia
content: Surely ELs can just be smart enough to not create a payload with more than the SSZ limit.

author: jtraglia
content: I feel like there are two options: (1) queue deposits and sig verifications + raise the SSZ limit or (2) lower the SSZ limit and make ELs aware of this.

author: jtraglia
content: But you are saying: (3) just raise the SSZ limit and hope it's not an issue because it's expensive.

author: jtraglia
content: Isn't 200m gas limit the _floor_? I thought we were shooting for like 400m gas in the near future.

author: jtraglia
content: <@808969530608451584> what happens in your kurtosis devnet when there are 8192 new builder deposits? How long does it take to recover?

author: terencechain
content:
<@755590043632140352>  <@592004585506340885> whats interesting here is with deferred payload processing, `n+1`'s proposer gets the burden, vs before it's the `n` 's builder gets the burden. That means as a rational builder.. i could include a few valuate mev txs.. then fill rest of my block with builder deposits and try to get `n+1` proposer to miss its slot.. and try to win for `n+2`. Something weird like that..

keep in mind the attack here doesnt have to be invalid deposit. In the happy case.. all valid deposits.. my M1 Max already takes 800ms to batch verify 8192 deposits. 90% of them are pubkeyu deserailziation and subgroup check. I can shred off 100ms by not doing linearly scan of `state.builders` for every `add_builder_from_deposit` call , but that's not enough if we are are thinking to move attestation deadline to 2.5-3s

author: terencechain
content: i shred off another 100ms by parallel pubkey deserlization. I dont think prysm can make it faster than 500-600ms to verify 8192 deposits. 99% of the overhead is cgo here 🙁

author: jtraglia
content: Which BLS library are you using? blst or a pure-go one? Also, I bet your M1 Max is more powerful than most mainnet nodes.

author: terencechain
content:
blst.. with the go bindings. 
exactly.. if something takes 500ms on m1 max.. probably takes 1-2s on a med to low end NUC

author: terencechain
content: constraint the number of deposits consensus block must have may be the only viable path

author: m.kalinin
content:
i think we need a second queue, <@592004585506340885> you closed your PR too early 😄 
the second queue makes sense as the first one has the activation queue limitation, i don't think we want to have a different scan over the same queue as it's the complexity while the semantics similar to the first queue. I believe all the checks should be done upon dequeuing then there shouldn't be any conflict as far as i can think of

author: m.kalinin
content: i'll try to open a PR

author: m.kalinin
content:
> Whenever there can be a race we need to validate signatures at the insertion point, which defeats the purpose entirely of the extra queue.
Why at the insertion point?

author: parithosh
content: wouldn't it be easier to enforce the limit on the EL block building logic instead of a second queue? if we have over x deposits in a block then consider it invalid?

author: m.kalinin
content: it depends on the limit

author: m.kalinin
content: if it's a 512 it might still be high. If it's 16 then it's probably fine from sig verification perspective, but quite restrictive from the deposit inclusion perspective

author: barnabasbusa
content: with 16 we can easily see someone just hogging the queue all the time

author: barnabasbusa
content: with 512 the hogging becomes a lot more expensive

author: m.kalinin
content: are we fine with 512 sig verifications per block?

author: m.kalinin
content: Alternative PR: https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5221

author: barnabasbusa
content: 16 is extremly limiting

author: barnabasbusa
content: that means with 32 eth you can hog that queue non stop?

author: barnabasbusa
content: you setup a dummy bot and keep depositing 16 new builders, then exit them in the following epoch then rinse and repeat

author: barnabasbusa
content: also none of this fix the top up deposit issue

author: m.kalinin
content: what is the issue?

author: barnabasbusa
content: where we still gonna be shit out of luck if anyone does  8193x1ETH top up requests

author: m.kalinin
content: ah, that's a different thing

author: barnabasbusa
content: yeah

author: barnabasbusa
content: but if we end up limiting all deposit types on the EL side, then that could solve the same issues

author: barnabasbusa
content: if we say on the EL side we want at most 512 deposits bundled in a deposit, then we don't need a queue, don't need to worry about top up deposits reaching 8193+, or how much time it would take for signature verification to take place and the mempool could take care of the priority of such deposits

author: m.kalinin
content: `MIN_BUILDER_WITHDRAWABILITY_DELAY=64`, so it's there going to be 64 epochs to wait till you can withdraw, if you don't want to wait then you have to have a budget for 64 epochs, which is `64*32*MAX_DEPOSIT_REQUESTS_PER_PAYLOAD` ETH, looks quite expensive. What is the benefit of such an attack?

author: barnabasbusa
content: thats only 1024 ETH

author: m.kalinin
content: 16 per block

author: barnabasbusa
content: `MAX_PENDING_BUILDER_DEPOSITS_PER_EPOCH`

author: barnabasbusa
content: this is the new flag you proposed

author: barnabasbusa
content: per_epoch

author: m.kalinin
content: this is a different thing, if we limit to 16 per payload, not to 512 without introducing a separate queue

author: potuz
content: Pawel explained why this is not possible: you can triviallly dos the builder because it only finds out that the tx is not includable after full simulation, and this doesn't cost any money

author: potuz
content: Have you read my review? How do you deal with the race without the signature verification?

author: barnabasbusa
content: could we introduce a new tx type for deposits? Then limit them by that? Similar to how we filter blob txs due to their new tx type

author: potuz
content: I think the right thing to do is what I said originally to do: add a longer exit churn for builders: this makes the attack actually cost money at least

author: potuz
content: Limiting at the EL seems not to be simple

content: how are we limiting blob txs?

author: potuz
content: Oh you mean statically count that type of txs

content: yeah

author: potuz
content: Yeah that would work

author: potuz
content: Adding a new tx type is not trivial I think

author: barnabasbusa
content: how would clients be able to 'ban' legacy deposit format?

author: potuz
content: I think the best solution to this issue is if we're able to handle these deposits. Notice that in theory the CL *can* optimize to precompute these signature verifications before the next block arrives

author: potuz
content: And only limit these by gas limit

author: potuz
content: And anyway we need to make the exit churn larger to make this attack more expensive
